content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

ROBERT R. WELLINGTON
857 CASS STREET, SUITE D
MONTEREY, CALIFORNIA 93940
(408) 373-8733

ROBERT R. WELLINGTON
KENNETH D. BUCHERT

FACSIMILE
(408) 373-7106

COPY VIA FACSIMILE

December 27, 1991


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95815

Re: City of Marina; Request
    for Advice Letter

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am the Assistant City Attorney for the City of Marina and,
as I discussed with John Wallace of your office today, I am re-
questing advice on behalf of three members of the Marina City
Council. The question on behalf of all three members is whether
any are disqualified from participating in decisions regarding
development of the Marina Landing Shopping Center.

Facts

The Marina City Council shall, over the next several months,
make a series of decisions regarding the Marina Landing Shopping
Center. These decisions will involve such issues as formation of
an assessment district, property exchanges between the landowner
and the City, funding of off-site improvements, and if actions of
the Planning Commission regarding land use entitlements or the
Environmental Impact Report are appealed, decisions in those
matters as well. No Council Member owns land directly involved in
the project or has or will have a business interest in the project.
However, three members own and live in single family dwellings
within 2,500 feet of the project area as follows:

a) Mayor Edith Johnsen's residence is approximately 950 feet
   from the project site.

b) Council Member Linda Horning's residence is approximately
   2,400 feet from the project site.


[illegible]

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.        December 27, 1991        Page 2


     c)  Council Member Lloyd Yates' residence is approximately
2,400 feet from the project site.

     The City of Marina, including the portion of Port Ord within
the City limits, contains 3,041 single family units and 5,220 mul-
tiple family units for a total of 8,261 dwelling units. Of that
total, 976 single family units are within 2,500 feet of the project
site representing 32% of the single family dwelling units within
the City. There are also 901 multiple family dwelling units within
2,500 feet of the project site, meaning that of the total of 8,261
dwelling units within the City, 1,877 are within 2,500 feet of the
project site, or 23%. This latter figure is somewhat deceptive,
however, because 2,082 dwelling units are owned by the federal
government on Fort Ord and any decision of the Marina City Council
cannot possibly effect the financial interest of the residents of
those dwelling units. This means the dwelling units within 2,500
feet of the project site actually represent 30% (1,877 of 6,179) of
the total which could be economically effected by the decisions.

     Using the latest United States census figures that the average
population in the City is 3.0289 persons per single family dwelling
and 3.2998 persons per multiple family dwelling unit, it is esti-
mated that 5,929 people live within 2,500 feet of the project.
This represents 22.4% of the total population (including Fort Ord)
of 26,436, or 35% of the population, excluding Fort Ord, of 16,973.

     All three Council members have stated in an open Council
meeting their opinions that the shopping center will not have any
financial effect on the value of their individual properties, and
the City has no reason to believe to the contrary. This office has
no information to lead us to believe that even if there were such
an effect that it would be different from that of any other owner
of a single family dwelling unit in the City, or particularly
within 2,500 feet of the project site.

     The City Council is scheduled to consider this shopping center
at a joint meeting with the Planning Commission on the evening of
January 6, 1992. Because this shopping center is of the utmost im-
portance to the residents of the City in view of the economic
impact on the City of the upcoming closure of Fort Ord, all Council
members feel their participation in the decisions on the shopping
center is necessary and desirable. If at all possible, we would
appreciate your reply not later than 5:00 P.M., January 6, 1992, by
facsimile (FAX) if necessary.

     If I can provide any further information or answer your
questions please feel free to contact me.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.                December 27, 1991                Page 3


                              Very truly yours,

                              [signature]
                              Kenneth D. Buchert
                              Assistant City Attorney
                              City of Marina

KDB:pb
cc:  City Council
     City Manager
     City Planning Director

sections.facts:
The Marina City Council shall, over the next several months, make a series of decisions regarding the Marina Landing Shopping Center. These decisions will involve such issues as formation of an assessment district, property exchanges between the landowner and the City, funding of off-site improvements, and if actions of the Planning Commission regarding land use entitlements or the Environmental Impact Report are appealed, decisions in those matters as well. No Council Member owns land directly involved in the project or has or will have a business interest in the project. However, three members own and live in single family dwellings within 2,500 feet of the project area as follows:

a) Mayor Edith Johnsen's residence is approximately 950 feet from the project site.

b) Council Member Linda Horning's residence is approximately 2,400 feet from the project site.

It appears that the image is extremely overexposed, making it very difficult to read or see any details. If you have another scan or a higher quality image, please provide it for better results.

c) Council Member Lloyd Yates' residence is approximately 2,400 feet from the project site.

The City of Marina, including the portion of Fort Ord within the City limits, contains 3,041 single family units and 5,220 multiple family units for a total of 8,261 dwelling units. Of that total, 976 single family units are within 2,500 feet of the project site representing 32% of the single family dwelling units within the City. There are also 901 multiple family dwelling units within 2,500 feet of the project site, meaning that of the total of 8,261 dwelling units within the City, 1,877 are within 2,500 feet of the project site, or 23%. This latter figure is somewhat deceptive, however, because 2,082 dwelling units are owned by the federal government on Fort Ord and any decision of the Marina City Council cannot possibly effect the financial interest of the residents of those dwelling units. This means the dwelling units within 2,500 feet of the project site actually represent 30% (1,877 of 6,179) of the total which could be economically effected by the decisions.

Using the latest United States census figures that the average population in the City is 3.0289 persons per single family dwelling and 3.2998 persons per multiple family dwelling unit, it is estimated that 5,929 people live within 2,500 feet of the project. This represents 22.4% of the total population (including Fort Ord) of 26,436, or 35% of the population, excluding Fort Ord, of 16,973.

All three Council members have stated in an open Council meeting their opinions that the shopping center will not have any financial effect on the value of their individual properties, and the City has no reason to believe to the contrary. This office has no information to lead us to believe that even if there were such an effect that it would be different from that of any other owner of a single family dwelling unit in the City, or particularly within 2,500 feet of the project site.

The City Council is scheduled to consider this shopping center at a joint meeting with the Planning Commission on the evening of January 6, 1992. Because this shopping center is of the utmost importance to the residents of the City in view of the economic impact on the City of the upcoming closure of Fort Ord, all Council members feel their participation in the decisions on the shopping center is necessary and desirable. If at all possible, we would appreciate your reply not later than 5:00 P.M., January 6, 1992, by facsimile (FAX) if necessary.

If I can provide any further information or answer your questions please feel free to contact me.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.                                                                 December 27, 1991
